description: Link-code redemption is a non-atomic SELECT-then-DELETE: findValidLinkSub (a plain SELECT gated only on expires_at > NOW()) and the later deleteLinkCode are separate statements with no row lock, no DELETE ... RETURNING, and no check of the delete's affected-row count. Two concurrent `/treasuryapi ui link <code>` invocations (both routes are @Async, so they run on separate command threads) can both pass the validity check before either deletes the row, so a code intended to be single-use can be redeemed twice. Because a code maps to one keycloak_sub, the second redeemer's upsertIdentity overwrites the first's identity row (ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE player_uuid_bin = VALUES(...)), so whoever wins the race owns the explorer identity for that Keycloak subject. A shared/leaked code is thus redeemable by an unintended player, silently rebinding the explorer login to a different Minecraft account.
recommendation: Make redemption atomic: have deleteLinkCode return the affected-row count (or use a DELETE ... WHERE code=? AND expires_at>NOW() that also yields the sub, e.g. via a conditional DELETE guarded then SELECT, or a single UPDATE ... SET redeemed_by claim pattern) and only proceed with upsertIdentity when exactly one row was claimed by this caller. Reject (link.invalid) when the claim affects 0 rows so a concurrent/second redemption cannot rebind the identity.
verification_note: Snippet matches doLink verbatim at UiAccessHandler.java:111-133. ExplorerUiMapper confirms the two statements are non-atomic and unlockable: findValidLinkSub (line 31-32) is a plain SELECT gated only on expires_at > NOW() with no FOR UPDATE; deleteLinkCode (line 34-35) is a separate DELETE returning void, so no affected-row count is checked; upsertIdentity (line 37-41) uses ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE player_uuid_bin = VALUES(...), so a second redemption on the same keycloak_sub overwrites the identity binding. The ui link route is @Async (TreasuryAPICommand.java:161), so two concurrent invocations run on separate command threads and can both pass findValidLinkSub before either deletes the row, allowing a single-use code to be redeemed twice and rebinding the explorer login to a different Minecraft account. The described race and its consequence hold. Minor severity is fair: real identity-binding hijack but with tight preconditions (attacker must know a valid, unexpired, unredeemed code issued to a specific web user, and must win a narrow SELECT-before-DELETE window) and low blast radius (a game-economy explorer login, not the money ledger).

description: doUserRemove and doUserList resolve the target via resolveUuid, which calls Bukkit.getOfflinePlayer(name) with no hasPlayedBefore()/isOnline() guard — unlike doUserAdd (lines 79-83), which deliberately rejects never-seen names to avoid fabricating a deterministic offline UUID (ADT-38). For a mistyped name, removeRole matches 0 rows and the operator is told "{player} did not have the {role} role" and listRoles returns an empty list rendered as "player (no elevated roles)". Both outcomes are harmless (no wrong data written) but the message misstates the cause: the real issue is the name was never seen, yet the operator is told the (fabricated) account simply lacks the role. This is a confusing-behaviour inconsistency with the add path rather than a data defect.
recommendation: For consistency with doUserAdd, apply the same hasPlayedBefore()/isOnline() guard in doUserRemove and doUserList (or in resolveUuid) and emit treasuryapi.ui.user.unknown-player when the name was never seen, so the operator learns the name was unrecognised rather than being told a fabricated account lacks the role.
